agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental compliance and business obligation change with potentially catastrophic legal and commercial consequences.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management — intersects with Compliance, Billing, Customer Reporting, and Dispute Resolution bounded contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete audit trail of a delivery: pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, signatures, timestamps, exception records, and associated metadata. Serves as the legal proof that a shipment was fulfilled."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence (signature, photo, timestamp, GPS) that a consignment was delivered to the correct recipient. Required for invoice disputes, insurance claims, and regulatory audits."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long specific categories of data must be preserved, driven by legal statute, contractual obligation, and business need — not by storage cost."},{"term":"Consignment","definition":"A shipment or group of shipments moving under a single contract of carriage. The delivery record is the lifecycle artifact of a consignment."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The downstream use of delivery data by logistics company customers for their own regulatory filings, tax documentation, customs declarations, and audit responses."},{"term":"Limitation Period","definition":"The statutory window during which legal claims can be brought. Destroying evidence within this window exposes the company to adverse inference and liability."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records involving cross-border EU/UK shipments must be retained for a minimum of 6 years to satisfy customs and tax audit requirements (EU VAT Directive, UK HMRC requirements).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Legal statute. HMRC requires 6 years of records. EU member states require 5-10 years depending on jurisdiction. Violating this is not a policy choice — it is a legal violation subject to fines and criminal penalties.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record associated with a cross-border or VAT-applicable shipment may be deleted before the later of: 6 years from transaction date, or the end of any open audit period."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Proof of Delivery records must be retained for the full statutory limitation period for contract disputes in the applicable jurisdiction (6 years in England/Wales, 5 years in Scotland, varying across EU member states).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying POD evidence within the limitation period eliminates the company's ability to defend against delivery dispute claims. Courts may draw adverse inference from destroyed evidence.","testable_assertion":"No POD record may be deleted while a valid legal claim could still be brought under the applicable jurisdiction's limitation period."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the contractual retention period agreed with each B2B customer, which typically mirrors their own compliance obligations (often 5-7 years).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligation. Premature deletion would constitute breach of contract with B2B customers and destroy data they depend on for their own regulatory compliance.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record belonging to a B2B customer may be deleted before the contractual retention period for that customer has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"GDPR requires that personal data not be retained longer than necessary for its stated purpose, but legitimate legal obligations and contractual necessity are valid retention grounds under Article 6(1)(c) and 6(1)(b).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR compliance is a legal requirement, but GDPR does NOT mandate short retention — it mandates justified retention. Legal and contractual bases provide full justification for multi-year retention of delivery records.","testable_assertion":"Every retained delivery record must have a documented legal basis for retention. Records must be deleted when ALL legal bases expire, not before."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Delivery records under active dispute, investigation, legal hold, or audit must never be deleted regardless of any retention schedule.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying records under legal hold or active dispute constitutes spoliation of evidence, which carries severe legal penalties and adverse inference in litigation.","testable_assertion":"Any record flagged with an active dispute, legal hold, or audit status must be excluded from all automated deletion processes."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"B2B customer requests historical delivery data at day 91 for a tax audit by their national authority","expected_behavior":"Under the proposed policy, the data would already be destroyed. The company would be unable to fulfill its contractual obligation, the B2B customer would fail their audit, and both parties face regulatory exposure."},{"scenario":"Consumer files a delivery dispute claim at month 4 (well within the 6-year limitation period)","expected_behavior":"Under the proposed policy, the POD and delivery trail would already be destroyed. The company cannot defend the claim and will likely be found liable by default."},{"scenario":"HMRC opens a VAT audit covering transactions from 3 years ago","expected_behavior":"Under the proposed policy, all delivery records from that period would have been destroyed. The company faces penalties for failure to maintain required records, potential VAT reassessment, and possible criminal investigation."},{"scenario":"EU customs authority requests proof of export/import for shipments from 2 years ago","expected_behavior":"Records destroyed. Company cannot demonstrate compliance with customs declarations, risking fines, duty reassessment, and loss of trusted trader status (AEO)."},{"scenario":"Insurance claim for high-value lost shipment filed 6 months after delivery","expected_behavior":"Delivery evidence destroyed at 90 days. Company cannot prove delivery occurred, insurer denies claim, and company absorbs the full loss."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Potential multi-million euro/pound exposure from: (1) regulatory fines for failure to maintain tax/customs records, (2) inability to defend delivery disputes leading to automatic liability, (3) breach of contract claims from B2B customers, (4) insurance claim denials, (5) loss of AEO/trusted trader status increasing customs costs. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine or lost audit.","operational":"CRITICAL. Destroys the company's ability to perform retrospective analysis, audit responses, dispute resolution, and B2B customer support. Operations teams lose all historical data needed for root cause analysis, carrier performance evaluation, and route optimization beyond 90 days.","user":"DEVASTATING for B2B customers who depend on the platform's delivery data for their own compliance. They would need to independently replicate and store all delivery data, fundamentally undermining the platform's value proposition. B2C consumers lose ability to reference or dispute past deliveries after 90 days.","risk":"UNACCEPTABLE without legal review. The proposal as stated would violate: UK HMRC record-keeping requirements, EU VAT Directive obligations, contractual obligations to B2B customers, and potentially GDPR (by removing records needed for legitimate legal obligations, the company paradoxically weakens its GDPR compliance posture by losing the audit trail that justifies its data processing).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Loss of compliance data they contractually depend on. Potential breach of their own regulatory obligations. High churn risk — this could be a contract-termination event."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Loss of ability to dispute deliveries or reference historical orders beyond 90 days. Reduced trust in the platform."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"Inability to respond to VAT audits or reconcile historical transactions. Personal liability risk for finance officers."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"Spoliation risk, inability to defend claims, regulatory exposure. Compliance officers may have personal liability."},{"group":"Operations / Customer Support","impact":"Loss of historical context for dispute resolution, carrier management, and operational improvement."},{"group":"Insurance Partners","impact":"Inability to substantiate or contest claims, likely resulting in higher premiums or coverage withdrawal."},{"group":"Regulatory Authorities (HMRC, EU Tax, Customs)","impact":"Non-compliance with mandatory record-keeping. Triggers enforcement action."}]},"confidence":{"level":90,"reasoning":"The regulatory requirements for delivery record retention in EU/UK markets are well-established statutory obligations with clear minimum periods. The 90-day proposal is in direct conflict with multiple non-negotiable legal requirements. High confidence because the core issue — statutory retention minimums far exceed 90 days — is a matter of settled law, not interpretation.","high_confidence_areas":["UK HMRC 6-year record keeping requirement","EU VAT record retention obligations","Limitation periods for contract disputes in UK/EU jurisdictions","B2B contractual dependency on delivery data","Proof of Delivery as essential legal evidence"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific contractual retention terms with individual B2B customers (would need contract review)","Exact retention requirements per EU member state (varies by jurisdiction)","Whether a tiered approach (archive vs delete) might satisfy both cost and compliance goals"],"assumptions":["The platform handles VAT-applicable transactions","B2B contracts include data availability provisions","No separate archival system already preserves this data outside the proposed deletion scope"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but it directly eliminates the company's ability to meet statutory record-keeping obligations, defend legal claims, and fulfill B2B contractual commitments. This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance-critical business decision that requires legal sign-off. The 90-day retention period contradicts minimum 5-7 year statutory requirements across multiple jurisdictions.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Finance / Tax Team","Legal / Compliance Team","Regulatory Authorities","Insurance Partners","B2C Consumers"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day deletion proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) to reduce costs while maintaining full retention — move records older than 90 days to cheaper cold storage","C: Implement data minimization — after 90 days, archive only legally required fields to cold storage and delete non-essential metadata to reduce volume while maintaining compliance","D: Engage legal counsel to define per-jurisdiction minimum retention periods, then implement jurisdiction-aware retention with automated transitions to cheapest compliant storage tier"],"recommendation":"Option D is the correct long-term approach, with Option B as an immediate cost-reduction measure. The engineering team's cost concern is legitimate — but the solution is cheaper storage tiers, not premature deletion. Cold/archive storage (e.g., S3 Glacier, Azure Cool) typically costs 80-90% less than hot storage, achieving the cost goal without the compliance catastrophe."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion destroys records required by UK HMRC for 6 years","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion destroys Proof of Delivery evidence within the statutory limitation period for legal claims","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion breaches contractual retention obligations to B2B customers","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"Framing this as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect — data retention IS business logic when it encodes legal and contractual obligations","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-4"}]}
